title: Math & Stats Intuition for Leaders
pitchHtml: You will never derive a gradient in a client meeting. But you will sit across from a data scientist or a vendor who quotes you an "average", a "95% accuracy", a "statistically significant lift", and a "the model just learns the function" — and your job is to know which of those is load-bearing and which is a polite way of saying trust me. This module gives you the intuition to follow a data-science conversation, smell nonsense, and make the call — not the maths to do the work yourself. It is the deliberately-lightened distillation of the AI Scientist path's Mathematical Toolkit and Statistical Learning Theory: keep the judgment, drop the proofs.

items.html: Read a summary statistic ("the average user…") and know when it's lying to you — and recognise mean, variance, std and the 68-95-99.7 rule when they're quoted
Use base rates (Bayes' theorem) to puncture a scary-sounding "95% accurate" claim in your head, on the spot
Frame a decision between options with expected value, out loud, in a meeting
Tell the difference between correlation found and causation proven — and price the gap
Explain what "a model" is, what "training" does, and what "loss" means, without equations
Recognise overfitting vs underfitting from symptoms, and know why more data / simpler models help
Challenge a "significant result" with the three questions that separate real from noise

subsections.title: Thinking in Distributions, Not Single Numbers

blocks.html: The single most useful upgrade to a leader's statistical instinct: when someone gives you one number, ask what the shape behind it looks like. A summary collapses a whole distribution into a point, and the collapse is where the lying happens.

StatisticPlain meaningWhen it liesWhat to ask for instead
Mean (average)Total ÷ countWhen data is skewed or has outliers — one whale drags itThe median, or the full spread
MedianThe middle value; half above, half belowWhen you actually care about the total (revenue, cost)The mean too
Spread (std dev / range)How much values vary around the centreAlmost never — its absence is the lie"What's the variation, not just the average?"
Tail / outliersThe rare extreme valuesWhen they're the whole story (fraud, outages, top customers)"What happens in the worst 5%?"

The classic trap: "average" lies whenever the distribution is skewed. Average revenue per user in a freemium product is meaningless — a handful of whales sit far out in the tail and pull the mean up, while the typical (median) user pays nothing. "Average time to resolve a ticket is 2 hours" can hide that most close in 10 minutes and a long tail drags out for days. The average is a real number describing nobody.

blocks.title: Worked example
blocks.html: Ten support tickets close in (minutes): 5, 6, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 426 (one ticket sat over a weekend). Mean ≈ 50 min — a number that describes none of the eleven. Median = 8.5 min — the honest "typical" experience. If your SLA is written against the mean, one bad weekend silently blows it; if a data scientist reports only the mean, you're being managed, not informed.
The three numbers that summarise a distribution are the mean (the centre), the variance (how spread out it is), and the standard deviation (the spread, in the data's own units):
μ=1n∑ixiσ2=1n∑i(xi−μ)2σ=σ2\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i x_i \qquad \sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i (x_i-\mu)^2 \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}μ=n1​i∑​xi​σ2=n1​i∑​(xi​−μ)2σ=σ2​

where xix_ixi​ are the data points, nnn is how many there are, μ\muμ ("mu") is the mean, σ2\sigma^2σ2 ("sigma-squared") is the variance, and σ\sigmaσ is the standard deviation. Use case: any time you want to say not just "the typical value" but "how much it varies" — SLA spread, revenue volatility, model-error consistency.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Variance is the average squared distance from the mean — squaring makes every deviation positive and punishes big misses harder, but it leaves you in "minutes-squared," which means nothing to anyone. Take the square root and you get the standard deviation, back in plain minutes (or euros, or whatever the data is) — which is the whole point of using std rather than variance when you talk to humans: it's the spread in the same units as the data, so "€40k average order, ±€55k std" instantly tells you the spread dwarfs the centre.

The normal distribution (the bell curve) is the default mental model for "things that vary around a centre by lots of small independent causes" — heights, measurement noise, sums of many small effects. It's a good default because so much really is roughly bell-shaped, and because most basic stats quietly assume it. Its single most useful property is the 68-95-99.7 rule: in a normal distribution, about 68% of values fall within 1 standard deviation of the mean, 95% within 2, and 99.7% within 3.

blocks.tex: P(\mu-\sigma \le X \le \mu+\sigma) \approx 68\% \quad P(\mu-2\sigma \le X \le \mu+2\sigma) \approx 95\% \quad P(\mu-3\sigma \le X \le \mu+3\sigma) \approx 99.7\%
blocks.html: P(μ−σ≤X≤μ+σ)≈68%P(μ−2σ≤X≤μ+2σ)≈95%P(μ−3σ≤X≤μ+3σ)≈99.7%P(\mu-\sigma \le X \le \mu+\sigma) \approx 68\% \quad P(\mu-2\sigma \le X \le \mu+2\sigma) \approx 95\% \quad P(\mu-3\sigma \le X \le \mu+3\sigma) \approx 99.7\%P(μ−σ≤X≤μ+σ)≈68%P(μ−2σ≤X≤μ+2σ)≈95%P(μ−3σ≤X≤μ+3σ)≈99.7%

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The normal curve says: most values huddle near the average, and extreme values are exponentially rare — the 68-95-99.7 rule is what "exponentially rare" looks like in practice, and it's why a "3-sigma event" is treated as a genuine alarm (under normality it should happen ~0.3% of the time). That makes the bell curve a great default for heights and measurement error — and dangerous for money, web traffic, file sizes, city sizes, and pandemics, which are heavy-tailed: the extremes are rare but not that rare (a "5-sigma" market move that the normal model says is once-in-a-million-years shows up every few years), and they dominate the totals. When a model or a plan assumes "normal" and reality is heavy-tailed, the surprises ("a once-in-500-years event, twice this decade") are baked in from the start. Ask: "are we assuming this is bell-shaped? What if the tail is fatter than that?"

You don't need probability theory; you need three instincts: base rates, conditional thinking, and expected value.

Base rates dominate — and intuition ignores them. This is the single most expensive blind spot in reading any test, screen, or classifier. The intuition pump is the medical test, and it transfers directly to fraud models, spam filters, security alerts, and disease screening.

blocks.title: why "95% accurate" can be mostly wrong
blocks.html: A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people. A test catches 99% of truly sick people and has only a 5% false-positive rate — sounds excellent. You test positive. What's the chance you're actually sick? Imagine 100,000 people. About 100 are sick → the test flags ~99 of them. The other 99,900 are healthy → at a 5% false-positive rate the test wrongly flags ~4,995 of them. So ~5,094 positives, of whom only ~99 are truly sick. Your probability of actually being sick given a positive test is about 99 / 5,094 ≈ 2%. A "99% accurate" test, applied to a rare condition, is wrong 98% of the time it fires. Nothing is broken — the rarity (base rate) swamps the accuracy.
The formula behind the count is Bayes' theorem — the rule for updating a belief (AAA = "sick") when new evidence arrives (BBB = "tested positive"):
P(A∣B)=P(B∣A) P(A)P(B)P(A\mid B)=\frac{P(B\mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}P(A∣B)=P(B)P(B∣A)P(A)​

where P(A∣B)P(A\mid B)P(A∣B) is what you actually want (chance of being sick given a positive test), P(B∣A)P(B\mid A)P(B∣A) is the test's catch rate (99%), P(A)P(A)P(A) is the base rate (the prior — 0.1%), and P(B)P(B)P(B) is the overall chance of a positive result (all positives, true and false). Plugging the example in: 0.99×0.0010.05094≈0.019\frac{0.99 \times 0.001}{0.05094} \approx 0.0190.050940.99×0.001​≈0.019 — the same ~2% the people-counting gave you. Use case: any "the test/model/alert said X, so X is true" claim — fraud flags, spam filters, security alerts, churn scores.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The whole result is driven by P(A)P(A)P(A), the base rate, sitting in the numerator: when the thing you're hunting is rare (P(A)P(A)P(A) tiny), even a near-perfect detector can't overcome it, because the flood of false positives from the huge healthy majority drowns the handful of true ones. Bayes just makes precise what the head-count showed — the prior dominates — which is why a team quoting accuracy without the base rate is hiding the term that decides the answer. You'll rarely compute it; you need to recognise that the answer cannot be read off the test's accuracy alone.

The leadership lesson: when the thing you're hunting is rare, even a good detector produces mostly false alarms. Any team pitching you a fraud/churn/anomaly model that quotes accuracy without telling you the base rate and the false-positive volume is hiding the only numbers that matter operationally (who has to chase all those false alarms?).

Conditional probability, in plain words, is just "the probability of A, given that we already know B." "20% of users churn" and "20% of users who contacted support twice churn" are different statements; conflating "probability of churn" with "probability of churn given this segment" is how people draw confident, wrong conclusions.

Expected value is how you choose between options under uncertainty — it's the one piece of maths worth saying out loud in a meeting because it forces the room to make its assumptions explicit. The expected value of an uncertain quantity XXX is the probability-weighted average of its outcomes:
E[X]=∑ipi xiE[X]=\sum_i p_i\, x_iE[X]=i∑​pi​xi​

where xix_ixi​ are the possible outcomes (a gain or a loss, in euros), pip_ipi​ is the probability of each, and the sum runs over every branch (the pip_ipi​ add to 1). Use case: any go/no-go under uncertainty — approve the loan or not, ship the feature or not, block the transaction or not. (In decision-speak this is the same as EV=∑iP(outcomei)×value(outcomei)\text{EV} = \sum_i P(\text{outcome}_i) \times \text{value}(\text{outcome}_i)EV=∑i​P(outcomei​)×value(outcomei​).)

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Don't compute it to three decimals — use it as a thinking discipline. A model that's right 80% of the time isn't "good" or "bad" in the abstract; it depends on what a right call is worth and what a wrong one costs. Approving a €200 loan with a 5% default risk: E[X]≈0.95×(gain)−0.05×(€200 loss)E[X] \approx 0.95 \times (\text{gain}) - 0.05 \times (\text{€200 loss})E[X]≈0.95×(gain)−0.05×(€200 loss). Blocking a legitimate €5,000 transaction to stop €50 of fraud is negative EV even though the fraud model "worked". Make the room name the probability and the payoff of each branch — most bad AI decisions die the moment those four numbers are on the whiteboard.

subsections.title: Correlation vs Causation (and Why It Costs You)

blocks.html:
Models find correlations — patterns of "when X is high, Y tends to be high." That is genuinely useful for prediction. It is not a licence to claim that changing X will change Y.

The number you'll most often see quoted is the (Pearson) correlation coefficient, rrr — a single value in [−1,1][-1, 1][−1,1] that measures how tightly two variables move together in a straight line:
r=∑i(xi−xˉ)(yi−yˉ)∑i(xi−xˉ)2 ∑i(yi−yˉ)2r = \frac{\sum_i (x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_i (x_i-\bar{x})^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_i (y_i-\bar{y})^2}}r=∑i​(xi​−xˉ)2​∑i​(yi​−yˉ​)2​∑i​(xi​−xˉ)(yi​−yˉ​)​

where xi,yix_i, y_ixi​,yi​ are the paired observations and xˉ,yˉ\bar{x}, \bar{y}xˉ,yˉ​ their means. Read it like a dial: r=+1r = +1r=+1 is a perfect upward line, r=−1r = -1r=−1 a perfect downward one, r=0r = 0r=0 no linear relationship. Use case: a fast first-pass "do these two things move together?" — feature screening, sanity-checking a driver before anyone builds on it.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: rrr measures only the strength and direction of a straight-line association, and it says nothing about cause. A high rrr can be pure coincidence or a hidden common cause (ice-cream sales correlate with drownings — both rise in summer); r≈0r \approx 0r≈0 can still hide a strong curved relationship the coefficient simply can't see. So a big rrr is a reason to investigate, never a reason to act. "Customers who use feature F churn less" (high rrr) does not mean pushing feature F reduces churn — maybe already-loyal users adopt F. Build a retention plan on that and you'll spend budget moving a number that doesn't move the outcome. The coefficient quantifies the correlation; it never upgrades it to causation.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: A predictive model answers "given what I observe, what's likely?" A causal claim answers "if I intervene and change this, what happens?" These are different questions and the second is much harder — it generally needs an experiment (an A/B test) or careful causal design, not just more data. When a stakeholder slides from "the model found that…" to "so if we do X, then Y," that's the exact sentence to interrupt: "is that a correlation the model saw, or a cause we've tested?"

The honest answer to "did this driver cause the result?" almost always requires a controlled experiment. That toolkit — A/B testing, confounders, and proving causation — gets its own treatment in Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation (the leader's version) and far deeper in AI Scientist 08 - Causal Inference & Experimentation. For now, hold the line: prediction is cheap, proof of cause is expensive, and conflating them burns budgets.

Strip away the mystique and a model is a function fitted from inputs to outputs. You hand it examples (square metres, location → house price), and "training" adjusts the function's internal parameters (its dials) so its predictions on those examples are as close as possible to the real answers.

  • Inputs / features: what the model sees (square metres, neighbourhood, age).
  • Parameters / weights: the dials the training process turns. A small model has dozens; a frontier LLM has hundreds of billions.
  • Loss: a single number measuring how wrong the model currently is (e.g. average squared error between predicted and actual price). Lower is better. Training = turning the dials to make the loss small.

How does it turn the dials? Gradient descent: feel the slope of the loss, take a step downhill, repeat. Each step nudges every parameter a little in the downhill direction:
θ←θ−η ∇L(θ)\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\, \nabla L(\theta)θ←θ−η∇L(θ)

where θ\thetaθ ("theta") are the parameters/dials, L(θ)L(\theta)L(θ) is the loss (how wrong the model is), ∇L(θ)\nabla L(\theta)∇L(θ) ("grad L") is the gradient — the slope of the loss, the direction that makes the error rise fastest — and η\etaη ("eta") is the learning rate, the step size. The minus sign is the whole trick: step against the slope to go downhill. Use case: this single rule is the engine that trains essentially every model, from linear regression to ChatGPT.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Picture standing on a foggy hillside (the loss landscape), wanting the lowest valley, able to feel only the slope under your feet. The gradient ∇L\nabla L∇L points straight uphill, so you move the other way — downhill — and that step must lower the loss as long as the step is small enough; do it over and over and you slide toward a valley. The learning rate η\etaη is your step size: too big and you bound clean over the valley and bounce around forever (the loss diverges); too small and you take all night to get down (and may stall in the first ditch you hit). "The model didn't converge" or "training was unstable" usually means η\etaη (or the landscape) was wrong — useful to recognise, never yours to hand-tune. You don't need the calculus; you need to know that training is iterative downhill stepping, it can get stuck, and it costs compute every step — which is why "just train a bigger model" is a line item, not a free wish.

That single idea — minimise a loss by stepping downhill — is the engine under almost everything, from linear regression to ChatGPT. The differences are the shape of the function and the number of dials, not the principle. (Deeper: AI Scientist Mathematical Toolkit.)

This is the concept that explains the most real-world model failures, and you can hold all of it as one picture: memorising vs generalising.

  • Underfitting: the model is too simple to capture the real pattern — it's wrong on the training data and on new data. (A straight line through obviously curved data.) It hasn't learned enough.
  • Overfitting: the model is so flexible it has memorised the training examples, including their noise and accidents. It looks brilliant on data it has seen and falls apart on data it hasn't. It learned too much — the wrong stuff.

blocks.title: the student analogy
blocks.html: Underfitting is the student who skimmed the textbook: shaky on practice problems and the exam alike. Overfitting is the student who memorised every worked example word-for-word: perfect on those exact problems, lost the moment the exam rephrases the question. You want the student who learned the method — mediocre-to-good on practice problems, and just as good on the exam. In ML the "exam" is data the model has never seen, which is why you always hold some data back to test on (see Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation).
There's a name for the two ways a model can be wrong. A model's total expected error on new data splits into three pieces:
Error=Bias2+Variance+Irreducible noise\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible noise}Error=Bias2+Variance+Irreducible noise

Bias is error from the model being too simple to capture the real pattern (underfitting — it's systematically off). Variance is error from the model being so sensitive it changes wildly with the particular training sample (overfitting — it chased the noise). Irreducible noise is the randomness in the world that no model can ever remove — the floor you cannot beat. Use case: a precise vocabulary for why a model underperforms and which lever to pull. (This is the standard decomposition — we state it, not derive it; the derivation lives in AI Scientist 02 - Statistical Learning Theory.)

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The two error terms pull against each other — that's the whole "tradeoff." Make the model simpler and bias rises while variance falls; make it more flexible and variance rises while bias falls. You can't drive both to zero at once, and you can never get below the irreducible-noise floor, so the goal is the balance that minimises their sum.

Plot error as you make the model more complex and you get exactly that U-shaped curve on new data: too simple, bias dominates and error is high (underfit); add the right complexity, error falls; keep adding, and variance takes over as the model starts memorising noise (overfit). The bottom of the U — where bias and variance balance — is the sweet spot. You don't need to derive the decomposition; you need the shape and the names.

blocks.text:
error on NEW data
   │ \                              /
   │  \                            /
   │   \                          /
   │    \____________      ______/
   │         underfit  \__/  overfit
   │                  (sweet spot)
   └─────────────────────────────────► model complexity

blocks.html:
Symptom you'll hearLikely diagnosisWhat helps
"Great on our data, bad in production"OverfittingMore/representative data; a simpler model; regularisation; proper validation
"It's mediocre even on the training data"UnderfittingA richer model; better features; let it train longer
"Accuracy was 99% in the lab"Overfitting or leakage or a rare-event base-rate trick (§2)Demand a test on held-out, realistic data

The three levers a leader should recognise — more (representative) data, regularisation, simpler models — all push the same way: they stop the model memorising noise and force it to generalise. "Regularisation" just means penalising needless complexity so the model prefers a simpler explanation; it's the mathematical version of Occam's razor (deeper: AI Scientist Statistical Learning Theory). When a team's answer to a bad model is always "make it bigger," they may be racing up the right-hand side of that U.

This is where you most often need to challenge a claim. Four ideas cover most of it.

Statistical significance & p-values, in plain language. A p-value answers one narrow question: "if there were really no effect, how surprising would a result this big be, by chance alone?" Stated precisely, it is the probability of seeing data at least this extreme assuming the null hypothesis H0H_0H0​ ("there is no real effect") is true:

blocks.tex: p = P(\text{data this extreme}\mid H_0\text{ true})
blocks.html: p=P(data this extreme∣H0 true)p = P(\text{data this extreme}\mid H_0\text{ true})p=P(data this extreme∣H0​ true)

The convention is to call a result significant when ppp falls below a threshold α\alphaα, usually 0.05. Small p (say < 0.05) = "this would be surprising if nothing were going on, so we doubt nothing-is-going-on." That's all it says. Note the direction of the conditioning: it's P(data∣H0)P(\text{data} \mid H_0)P(data∣H0​), not P(H0∣data)P(H_0 \mid \text{data})P(H0​∣data) — it is not the probability the claim is true, and it is not a measure of how big or important the effect is.

blocks.title: significant ≠ important
blocks.html: With a big enough sample, a trivial effect becomes "statistically significant." An A/B test on 10 million users can prove a button colour lifts conversion by 0.01% with p < 0.001 — real, repeatable, and commercially pointless. Conversely, a genuinely large effect on a tiny sample may fail significance simply because there wasn't enough data to rule out luck. Always ask two questions, not one: "is it real?" (significance) and "is it big enough to matter?" (effect size). A data scientist who reports a p-value but not the effect size has answered the less important question.

Sample size, intuitively: more data shrinks the role of luck. Conclusions drawn from 30 customers are mostly noise; the same pattern across 30,000 is far harder to dismiss as chance. Beware any strong claim ("this segment converts better!") resting on a handful of cases — and beware the mirror error of treating a massive sample as proof the effect matters (see above).

Sampling bias is the quiet killer, because no amount of data fixes it. If who you measured isn't who you'll act on, the numbers are confidently wrong. A satisfaction survey answered only by people who bothered to reply; a fraud model trained only on the fraud you already caught; a hiring model trained on who you historically hired. The sample doesn't represent the world the model will face.

blocks.title: the survey that lied
blocks.html: A product team reports "78% of users want feature X." It came from an in-app pop-up — answered only by today's engaged power users. The churned and the casual, the people whose feedback you most need, never saw it. The 78% is real and precise for power users, and useless as a read on the whole base. Big number, biased sample. Ask who's missing before you ask how many answered.

Claim you hearThe honest question back
"It's statistically significant.""What's the effect size — is it big enough to act on?"
"Accuracy is 95%.""On what data, and what's the base rate / false-positive volume?" (§2)
"Users prefer X.""Who did you sample, and who's missing?"
"The model found driver D matters.""Correlation, or a cause you've tested?" (§3)
"It worked great in testing.""On data the model had never seen before? Same as production?" (§5)
"The average is up.""What's the median and the spread — who's actually moving?" (§1)

Evaluation metrics (precision, recall, ROC/AUC, calibration, and which one to optimise for which business cost) are the deep version of "is it real and is it good" — they get their own module: Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation. Here, just know they exist and that accuracy alone is almost never the right metric.

This module's commercial value is unusual: it sells literacy and scrutiny, not a system. Two buyers exist — leaders who want to understand, and leaders who want a second opinion on someone else's numbers.

  • AI & Data Literacy Workshop for Leadership / COMEX — Problem: the executive team approves AI budgets and vendor deals but can't interrogate the claims, so they over-trust demos and under-fund data. Deliverable: a half-day to two-day workshop with a tailored slide deck, a one-page "Questions to Ask Any Data Scientist" leave-behind, and 2-3 worked examples built on their domain (their fraud rate, their churn numbers). Scope: 1-2 weeks to build and deliver; fixed price; highly repeatable IP. Who buys: COMEX, CDO/CTO, sometimes HR/L&D for a leadership-development line item.
  • Model-Claim Due-Diligence / Second Opinion — Problem: a vendor or an internal team is pitching "95% accuracy" / "proven 30% uplift" and a decision-maker wants a sanity check before signing or scaling. Deliverable: a short written review — does the claim survive base-rate, sample-size, sampling-bias, and significance-vs-importance scrutiny? — with a RAG-rated (red/amber/green) verdict and the precise follow-up questions to put to the vendor. Scope: 1-2 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: the budget-holder for the purchase, procurement, an investor doing technical diligence, a CFO who smells an overclaim.
  • Experiment / Stats Review (tie-in) — Problem: the team ran an A/B test or quotes a "significant" result and leadership isn't sure whether to roll it out. Deliverable: a methodology review — was it a fair test, big enough sample, correct significance and effect-size reading, any sampling bias — plus a go/hold/redesign recommendation. Scope: days to one week. Who buys: product/growth leadership. (Pairs naturally with Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation and AI Scientist 08 - Causal Inference & Experimentation.)

Buy-vs-build for the client: this is pure judgment as a service — there is nothing to build. The asset is your framework and your independence. What to charge for: the workshop as a packaged, repeatable offering; the due-diligence review as a fixed-price expert opinion (price it on the decision's value — sanity-checking a €500k vendor contract is worth far more than the day it takes). What NOT to promise: that you'll re-do their analysis or validate the model end-to-end inside a literacy/second-opinion scope — that's a full evaluation engagement (module 05), priced accordingly. Promise scrutiny and the right questions, not a re-derivation.
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
Spreadsheets (Excel, Google Sheets)Sketch a base-rate or EV calc live in a meetingPaid / FreeThe most under-rated stats tool; a 4-cell grid wins arguments
Jupyter / Google ColabReproduce a vendor's number to pressure-test itOSS / FreemiumYou won't write the model — you'll check one
Statistical calculators (A/B test, sample-size)Quick significance & power sanity checksMostly FreeMany free web tools; useful in the room, easy to misuse
LLM assistants (Claude, etc.)"Explain this statistical claim / spot the flaw"PaidA fast tutor and a draft-questions generator; verify its maths
Charting (any BI tool, matplotlib)See the distribution behind the averageMixedForcing a histogram on screen exposes most "average" lies
The real tool of this module is a set of questions and a mental model, not software. The deck and the checklist below are the deliverables.

SituationThe lighter move instead
Decision is small, cheap, and reversibleDon't model anything — pick, ship, watch, adjust
You just need the "typical" valueRead the median; skip the distributional deep-dive
The effect is obvious and huge (3× faster)You don't need a significance test to believe a 3× difference
A vendor's claim is non-critical / low-spendAsk the base-rate question once; don't commission a review
The team already runs disciplined experimentsTrust the process; spot-check, don't re-audit everything
You're tempted to demand more precisionAsk instead: "would a more precise number change the decision?" If no, stop.
The senior move is knowing when good enough is good enough. Statistical rigour is a cost; spend it where a wrong number is expensive, and nowhere else.

• Spiegelhalter — The Art of Statistics — exactly this module's altitude: intuition and honest reading, no proofs. The single best leader's read.
  • Kahneman — Thinking, Fast and Slow — why your gut ignores base rates (and what to do about it).
  • Huff — How to Lie with Statistics — short, old, evergreen; a field guide to the tricks you're defending against.
  • Wheelan — Naked Statistics — friendly, business-flavoured tour of the same ideas.

• The ASA's statement on p-values — the profession's own "here's what it does and doesn't mean," in plain language.
  • Any solid write-up of the base-rate / medical-test problem — internalise the one example and you'll never be fooled by "95% accurate" again.

• The maths behind training, loss, and optimisation → AI Scientist Mathematical Toolkit.
  • The theory behind overfitting, generalisation, and regularisation → AI Scientist Statistical Learning Theory.
  • Causation, properly → AI Scientist 08 - Causal Inference & Experimentation and Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation.
  • The hands-on coding companion (light touch) → ML Engineer Python for ML Engineering.

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Can I trust this number?"

items.html: Is this an average hiding a skewed distribution? Have I seen the median and the spread?
If it's a detector ("X% accurate"), do I know the base rate and the false-positive volume?
Is the claim correlation or a tested cause?
"Significant" — fine, but is the effect size big enough to act on?
Who was sampled, and who's missing? Could the sample be biased?
Was performance measured on data the model had never seen (held-out / production-like)?
Would a more precise number actually change my decision?

blocks.html:
• A single number with no distribution, no spread, no "out of how many."
  • "95% accurate" for a rare event, with no false-positive count.
  • A sentence sliding from "the model found…" straight to "so if we do…" (correlation dressed as cause).
  • "Statistically significant" quoted without an effect size.
  • A survey/result whose respondents clearly aren't the population you'll act on.
  • "It scored 99% in testing" with no held-out / production validation (overfitting or leakage).
  • "Just train a bigger model" as the answer to every problem.

subsections.title: Scoping Prompts (ask the data scientist / vendor)

blocks.html:
• "What does the distribution look like — show me the histogram, not just the mean."
  • "What's the base rate, and out of every 1,000 alerts, how many are false?"
  • "Is that a correlation the model saw, or a cause we've tested with an experiment?"
  • "It's significant — what's the effect size, and is it big enough to matter to the business?"
  • "Who's in this sample, and who's missing?"
  • "Was this measured on data the model had never seen? How close is that to production?"
  • "If this number were 10% different, would we make a different decision?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Hear "the average X" and reflexively ask for the median and the spread
Debunk a "95% accurate" claim on a rare event in your head, by counting people — no Bayes needed
Run an expected-value framing out loud to force a room's assumptions onto the table
Stop a stakeholder mid-sentence when correlation is being sold as causation
Explain training, parameters, loss, and the gradient-descent "step downhill" rule (and what the learning rate does) to a non-technical exec
Diagnose overfitting vs underfitting from the symptoms and name the lever that helps
Separate "is it real?" from "is it big enough to matter?" on any significance claim
